report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Taylor, thanks for sharing your answers with us. Your results paint a clear and interconnected picture, and the good news is that the patterns we're seeing tend to respond well when addressed at their root, especially with pregnancy preparation in mind.

### Your Energy Crisis Starts in Your Gut

A 2/10 energy level that stays flat all day, combined with diarrhea, food sensitivities, and brain fog, points strongly to gut dysfunction as the central driver. Here's the connection: when the gut microbiome is out of balance, bacterial byproducts like lipopolysaccharides (LPS) can enter the bloodstream and [directly impair mitochondrial energy production](https://rebootcenter.com/bust-endotoxins-and-recharge-your-mitochondria/), essentially turning down the power supply to your entire body. This creates a picture that looks exactly like yours: persistent, all-day fatigue that no amount of sleep seems to fix, alongside digestive trouble and cognitive fog.

Your post-meal crashes are a particularly telling clue. When someone is running on stress hormones (adrenaline, cortisol) to maintain even baseline energy, eating a meal signals safety to the body, and those hormones drop. The result is a crash. Meanwhile, gut bacteria can [feed on the incoming food and produce inflammatory compounds that further suppress energy metabolism](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7266694/), creating a double hit every time you eat.

```
Food Intake
    │
    ├──→ Stress hormones drop ──→ Energy crashes
    │
    └──→ Gut bacteria ferment food ──→ Endotoxin + inflammation
                                            │
                                            └──→ Mitochondria suppressed ──→ Fatigue, brain fog
```

### Your Diet May Be Feeding the Problem

Your current eating pattern, while well-intentioned, contains several elements that can worsen gut dysbiosis. Salads and raw vegetables are high in fiber that bacteria ferment, producing gas and inflammatory byproducts. Nuts are high in polyunsaturated fats, which promote intestinal inflammation and suppress metabolic rate. Together, these foods can perpetuate the exact cycle driving your symptoms.

This doesn't mean these are "bad" foods in general. It means that for a gut under stress, they add fuel to the fire. Shifting toward more easily digestible foods (well-cooked meals, simpler carbohydrates, saturated fats over seed oils) can reduce the bacterial burden and give your gut space to heal.

### Why This Matters Even More for Pregnancy

Your goal of optimizing before pregnancy makes addressing this especially important. Research shows that [maternal gut dysbiosis directly affects placental immune function and pregnancy outcomes](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-58533-8), and that [preconception microbiome optimization through targeted therapies significantly improves fertility and reduces complications](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12794029/) like miscarriage and preterm birth. Your baby's microbiome will be seeded by yours, so the work you do now has generational impact.

Beyond the microbiome itself, your body needs robust energy metabolism to support the enormous demands of pregnancy. Building that foundation now, by restoring gut function, reducing systemic inflammation, and supporting mitochondrial output, is one of the most meaningful things you can do in preparation.

---

This assessment is based on a brief quiz, so it captures patterns rather than the full picture. There's more to explore, particularly around your metabolic and hormonal status, but the gut-energy connection stands out as a clear starting point with the potential to shift many of your symptoms at once.
